(1) Fun Frontend Effects With GSAP3 - Who Is GSAP?

As a frontend dev, you build effects all the time. GSAP is one of the most useful tools for it — it lets you create all kinds of fun animations on the web with a pretty simple API. So let me write a series of GSAP tutorials.
GSAP stands for Green Sock Animation Platform — basically a tool that makes animation easier.

GSAP used to go by TweenMax. So you'll see a bunch of articles mentioning TweenMax, TweenLite, TimelineMax, TimelineLite — these were all different versions. The team eventually consolidated everything into GSAP and renamed the site.
What's special about GSAP? Short version: it's simple, performant, and broadly compatible. All the major frameworks support it — including PixiJS V5, which I covered in
my PixiJS V5 tutorial (article in Mandarin)
. So you can see compatibility is no joke.

GSAP also ships a ton of plugins — MotionPath Plugin, DrawSVG Plugin, ScrollToPlugin, and so on. With these you can build genuinely rich effects. Most are free, so you can use them without much worry. A few are paid, but not many.
This series focuses on GSAP3 basics — GSAP3 is the latest and officially recommended version, so we start there. All examples will be on CodePen, and you can grab them from
Each post will be on the shorter side — easier to digest. I'll include sample code + CodePen links so you can practice along.
Frontend devs build effects all the time, and GSAP is one of the most useful tools out there for it. The API is straightforward, so I figured I'd write a series of GSAP tutorials.
